headlines.geopolitics: Unclaimed Navy veteran given full military honors as community refuses to let him be buried alone
headlines.economy: Deputy AG announces new DOJ fraud division targeting 'insane' federal program abuse
headlines.domestic: Fox News poll shows voters expect AI transformation but find it barely useful today

metadata.daily_do: Check in on an elderly neighbor or veteran in your community — sometimes the smallest gesture prevents someone from being truly alone

clips.caption: When Duke gets emotional about military honor
Pringle explains why we honor unknown soldiers
Jax discovers government AI buying office chairs

script.text: Look, I don't care if you think I'm getting soft in my old age — when three hundred strangers show up to bury a man they never met, that's not just nice. That's America working the way it's supposed to.
Good morning. I'm Leo Barros. It's Monday, March 10th, and this is The Friction. Here's what happened while you weren't paying attention.

Geopolitics first. In Tennessee last week, hundreds of people showed up to give full military honors to Navy veteran Lonnie Wayman, who died with no known family to claim his remains. The VA had labeled him 'unclaimed,' but the community decided that was unacceptable.
Good.
Oh wow.

Veterans, community members, military personnel — they filled the sanctuary and stood outside. The VA rep said quote, 'We are able to claim our honorable veterans and provide them the dignity and honor that they have earned.'
I'm not crying, you're crying.
script.direction: trying to joke but actually moved

script.text: Economy. Deputy Attorney General Todd Blanche announced a new DOJ fraud division targeting what he calls 'insane' levels of federal program fraud. The example he gave — two Pennsylvania men who fraudulently received four million dollars for fake Minneapolis homeless shelters.
Four million? For fake shelters?
Two dudes from Philly driving to Minneapolis to rip us off. That's actually impressive in the worst possible way.
Blanche says they're going after any fraud over a thousand dollars as a felony. Quote, 'The fraud problem is insane.'
Finally, someone in government discovers that free money attracts criminals. Nobel Prize territory.
Domestic news. A new Fox News poll shows eighty-seven percent of voters expect AI to significantly change American life, but only forty-seven percent find it useful in their daily lives right now.
The future is coming, but it's running a bit late.

Eighty percent are extremely or very concerned about distinguishing real content from AI-generated content. And in a completely unrelated finding, seventy-four percent believe intelligent life exists elsewhere in the universe.
So we can't tell if a TikTok is real, but we're pretty sure aliens are out there. That tracks.
The aliens probably have better media literacy than we do.
Let's start with Tennessee. This story about Lonnie Wayman — it's not really about one veteran, is it? It's about something bigger.
It's about the promise we make. When someone serves, we tell them we'll take care of them. That doesn't end when they take off the uniform, and it doesn't end when they die.
But Duke, the fact that this story exists means the system failed, right? How do we have 'unclaimed' veterans in the first place?
The system failed the paperwork. The system didn't fail the man. Three hundred people showed up, Bree. That's the system working.
I love that they showed up, but why did they have to? We spend seven hundred and fifty billion dollars on defense every year, and we can't track whether our veterans have families?
We can track your Starbucks order from bean to cup, but we lose track of the guy who served on a submarine for four years.
The VA says they tried to locate family members. Sometimes there just aren't any. Sometimes families are estranged. Sometimes people outlive everyone they knew.

Right, which brings us to the bigger issue — social isolation among elderly veterans. We know this is a problem. We know suicide rates spike. We know depression rates are higher. So what's the systemic solution?
The systemic solution is what happened in Tennessee. Communities taking care of their own. You can't bureaucratize caring, Bree.
You can't rely on random acts of kindness for national policy either. What about the veterans who die alone in towns where nobody shows up?
Then those towns need to do better. But you start with the assumption that government is gonna fix loneliness, you're gonna be disappointed every time.
Duke, what did this story make you think about?

Honestly? My grandfather. He came back from Korea, never talked about it. Lived alone for the last fifteen years of his life. We visited, but not enough. And when he died, the VFW showed up. Guys he barely knew. But they knew the uniform.

Look, I don't care if you think I'm getting soft in my old age — when three hundred strangers show up to bury a man they never met, that's not just nice. That's America working the way it's supposed to.
I'm not arguing against that, Duke. I'm saying we shouldn't need to rely on it. What's the scalable version of three hundred strangers?
You want scalable? Every VFW post, every American Legion, every church, every neighborhood association. That's millions of people. That's more scalable than any government program.

Can I just say — the fact that we're even debating this proves we're good people? Like, most countries, you die alone, that's it. Game over. We're arguing about whether the response was systematic enough.
Jax makes a point. The story went viral because it was unusual, but also because it felt right. It confirmed something people want to believe about who we are.

Which is beautiful. And I want to live in that America. I just also want to live in the America where Lonnie Wayman's story doesn't happen in the first place because we have better systems for staying connected to our veterans.
Fair enough. But you build those systems from the bottom up, not the top down. And Tennessee just showed us how.
Professor, this made me think about the Tomb of the Unknown Soldier. There's something about how societies honor their military dead, especially when we don't know who they are.
Precisely, Leo. The parallel is exact and ancient. The Tomb of the Unknown Soldier wasn't invented by Americans — we inherited it from a much older human impulse.

After the Great War, every major nation faced the same problem Lonnie Wayman's community faced — thousands of dead soldiers whose bodies could never be identified, never sent home. Families with no grave to visit.

The British started it in Westminster Abbey in 1920. One unidentified soldier, buried with the highest honors, representing all the unknown dead. The inscription reads, 'They buried him among the kings because he had done good toward God and toward his house.'
Wait, among the kings?

Among the kings. The French followed within months. Arc de Triomphe, eternal flame, unknown soldier from Verdun. Then the Americans at Arlington in 1921. The ceremony drew over ninety thousand people.
Ninety thousand for someone they didn't know.

Exactly. Because the unknown soldier represents a compact between society and those who serve. We may not know your name, but we know your service. We may not know your story, but we know your sacrifice.
What happened in Tennessee last week was the same impulse. Lonnie Wayman became their unknown soldier. Not unknown because his identity was lost, but unknown because he was alone.
So those three hundred people were basically saying, 'We'll be your family.'

Precisely. And that impulse — to refuse to let service go unhonored — is as old as organized warfare itself. The Greeks had a word for it: 'kleos.' Glory that outlasts death. Honor that transcends the individual.
The Tomb of the Unknown Soldier isn't really about one soldier. It's about the promise that service will be remembered, even when the servant is forgotten. Tennessee kept that promise.
That's actually beautiful, Doc. And it makes Duke's point about community response. This isn't a policy failure — it's a community succeeding.
Three hundred people said, 'Not on our watch.' That's kleos.
Indeed. And it's worth noting — every year, millions of Americans visit Arlington to pay respects to soldiers they never knew. The impulse is universal. The execution is local.

Alright, time for the stuff that makes you question reality. First up, Palantir — you know, the AI company that helps the government spy on stuff — has now deployed their advanced artificial intelligence to solve the Pentagon's most pressing problem. Office chairs.
I'm sorry, what?

I am not making this up. They're using AI weaponry — literal military-grade artificial intelligence — to find office chairs for government staff. Because apparently, human beings cannot locate furniture without machine learning.
This is why China's gonna beat us. They're building hypersonic missiles. We're building hypersonic chair-finders.
Wait, but like, how hard is it to buy office chairs? There are literally stores for this.
Bree, you're thinking like a human. The government thinks like... well, like something that needs AI to find chairs. Moving on.

In the UK, a boss has been found guilty of workplace harassment for repeatedly shouting 'potato' at his Irish employee. The tribunal ruled that shouting 'potato' at someone because of their nationality is, quote, 'obviously offensive.'
I should think so.
The best part is that somewhere there's a lawyer who had to argue in court that yelling vegetables at people is inappropriate workplace behavior. Imagine that law school case study.
What's next, getting sued for calling someone a turnip?

Don't give them ideas. Speaking of giving people ideas, Louisiana has until Friday to post the Ten Commandments in every public school classroom. Because apparently, 'Thou shalt not kill' wasn't getting through via the regular curriculum.
This is gonna be a legal nightmare. Like, which version? Which translation? What font size?

Bree's asking the real questions. I'm imagining teachers frantically laminating Bible verses at Kinko's this week. 'Ma'am, you need the coveting one in fourteen-point font? That'll be four ninety-nine.'
The ACLU is probably already drafting lawsuits.

Oh, definitely. And finally, in Florida — because of course it's Florida — a drug dealer locked a man in a storage unit for months over an unpaid debt. The victim survived by eating ramen noodles and drinking water from a garden hose.
That's not a drug debt. That's a hostage situation.
Right? The guy basically lived in a ten-by-twelve concrete box for three months. That's not even a nice storage unit. That's like, barely above a medieval dungeon.
How is this not attempted murder?
It probably is, but it's Florida, so first they have to check if it violates any alligator ordinances. The drug dealer's been arrested, by the way. His defense? 'I thought he liked camping.'
I made that up, didn't you?
Totally made that up. But it sounds believable, which tells you everything about Florida.
Alright, time for the Daily Do. Today's takeaway comes from Tennessee. Here's something you can actually do.
Check in on an elderly neighbor or veteran in your community. I'm not talking about a government program or a big gesture. I'm talking about knocking on a door and asking how someone's doing.

The difference between being alone and being lonely is often just one person who notices. Lonnie Wayman's story is beautiful because three hundred people showed up. But it's also a reminder that maybe if one person had shown up while he was alive, the story might have been different.
My neighbor Mrs. Chen brings me tomatoes from her garden every summer. I bring her newspaper in when it rains. That's not policy. That's just being human.
My mom always says the best social program is a good neighbor. I used to think that was naive, but maybe it's just true.
Sometimes the smallest gesture prevents someone from being truly alone. That's not a small thing. That's everything.
As the Roman poet Terence wrote, 'I am human, and nothing human is alien to me.' Service to others doesn't end with the uniform, my friends. It begins there.

That's The Friction for Monday, March 10th. I'm Jax Mooney, reminding you that if the government needs AI to find office chairs, maybe we shouldn't trust them with the really hard stuff. But if three hundred strangers show up to honor a veteran, maybe we can trust each other. We'll see you tomorrow.
